blocks.text: What does the occluding contour tell us about solid
shape?
Jan J Koenderink
Department of Medical and Physiological Physics, Physics Laboratory, State University of Utrecht,
Princetonplein 5, 3584 CC Utrecht, The Netherlands
Received 16 May 1983, in revised form 15 December 1983
Abstract. A new theorem is discussed that relates the apparent curvature of the occluding contour
of a visual shape to the intrinsic curvature of the surface and the radial curvature. This theorem
allows the formulation of general laws for the apparent curvature, independent of viewing distance
and regardless of the fact that the rim (the boundary between the visible and invisible parts of the
object) is a general, thus twisted, space curve. Consequently convexities, concavities, or inflextions
of contours in the retinal image allow the observer to draw inferences about local surface geometry
with certainty. These results appear to be counterintuitive, witness to the treatment of the
problem by recent authors. It is demonstrated how well-known examples, used to show how
concavities and convexities of the contour have no obvious relation to solid shape, are actually
good illustrations of the fact that convexities are due to local ovoid shapes, concavities to local
saddle shapes.
1 Introduction
Three-dimensional Euclidean geometry is so complicated that many people experience
difficulties in obtaining veridical notions about unfamiliar spatial relationships.
Oddly enough, our visual systems automatically interpret some complicated spatial
configurations correctly, despite the fact that we may hold primitive and even
incorrect intellectual notions about geometrical relations. One such instance is the
interpretation of the visual contours of smooth curved objects. Here, even scientific
investigators appear as a rule to hold quite false and unfruitful notions. Despite this
fact (which will be illustrated later) even these investigators have quite normal visual
impressions; this is duly noted by them as a remarkable and as yet unexplained fact.
Because the geometry is incompletely or wrongly understood, it is thought that the
performance of the visual system must be due to (uncannily lucky) guesswork or the
use of prior information and cannot be based on causal relationships.
In this paper I intend to discuss some common fallacies in this respect, and to
replace them with factual observations. Some of the latter are not at all new, but
apparently only known to mathematicians (differential geometers) and not to visual
scientists. One result I will present is (as far as I could ascertain) new, and of
recurring applicability in matters of visual perception of solid shape. In order to
convince the reader of the need for a thorough analysis I need a few quotations, and
I have selected them from the well-known book of the late David Marr (1982),
which is so widely appreciated that I could hardly detract from its importance.
2 Preliminary
Consider smooth rigid bodies, for which the tangent plane is defined for every point
of the surface and moreover is a smooth (as many times differentiable as necessary)
function of position. From a given position in space all the rays through the vantage
point that are tangent to the surface of the body can be constructed. These rays
form a conical surface, the contour (or, equivalently, the curve through which this
conical surface cuts the unit sphere around the vantage point may be regarded as the
contour). The rays touch the object along a smooth curve on its surface, the rim.
Although the rim is smooth, the contour need not be: it can display cusps
(Koenderink and van Doorn 1976, 1979, 1984); moreover, the contour may intersect
itself, which the rim cannot do.
It is convenient to limit the discussion to generic situations. By this, I mean
situations which are stable under arbitrarily small deformations, for example, small
excursions of the vantage point or small deformations of the object. For instance,
a line may have infinitely many points in common with a plane, but this is not a
generic situation, for the smallest deformation of the plane will destroy it. The
generic tangent rays are of three types: either they graze the surface and meet it at
two infinitesimally close points (like a ray grazing a sphere); they graze the surface
and enter it, meeting the surface at three infinitesimally close points [then, the
contour cusps and the visible contour ends (Koenderink and van Doorn 1982)]; or,
finally, they may graze the surface at two places which are separated by a finite
distance (this leads to the so-called 'T-junctions'). Of the latter types of ray there
exist only finitely many (for a fixed vantage point), of the first type there is a
one-dimensional manifold: the cone mantle of the contour. The latter two types
thus are isolated singular rays of the contour.
The rim is not a plane curve, it is also not everywhere perpendicular to the visual
direction (for example, at the cusps of the contour the rim runs along the ray). It is
thus a space curve and generically has an everywhere positive curvature and finitely
many isolated points of vanishing torsion (points at which the 'helicity' changes from
a right-handed to a left-handed screw sense, or vice versa). Thus it is strictly senseless
to speak of inflections of the rim, although it would be a valid question to ask for
its torsion zeros. The contour can be understood as a spherical curve, thus it makes
good sense to speak of its inflections.
3 Inflections of the contour and inflections of the surface
Even in the absence of a metric or a coordinate system, it makes sense to speak of
the convexities and concavities (and thus inflections) of the contour. Marr (1982)
notes these features as the only ones that are straightforwardly available, rightly I
think. However, he adds (p 221): "In general, of course, points of inflexion in a
contour need have no significance for the surface". This is just not true. Marr
illustrates his contention with a snake whose body varies in distance from the vantage
point, then notes that convexities belong to near, concavities to far parts (see
figure 3, upper part). Whereas this is a valid observation, the conclusion is wrong:
the convex parts of the contour bound synclastic surface patches, the concave parts
anticlastic surface patches (to be defined below). The confusion probably arises
because the rim (called "contour generator" by Marr) moves over the body when you
move the vantage point. Thus the simile with a bent wire that can be viewed from
different positions is wrong because the space curve itself changes when the observer
moves.
Consider the facts about the curvature of the contour. First, consider the case of
a distant vantage point. Suppose one views a rim point R from a direction \(\xi\). The
following are constructed: the normal plane \(\boldsymbol{\nu}\), defined by the normal to the surface
at R and the visual direction \(\xi\), and the plane of projection \(\pi\) perpendicular to \(\xi\).
Then two curves defined through these entities may be regarded: the curve \(\lambda\) in
which the normal plane cuts the surface, and the curve \(\mu\) along which the cylinder
of tangent rays parallel to \(\xi\) cuts the plane of projection \(\pi\) (figure 1).
The radii of curvature of these curves at R or its projection may be measured.
The curvature

\[
\boldsymbol{K}_{\mathbf{r}}=1 / r_{\mathbf{r}},
\]
where \(r_{\mathbf{r}}\) is the radius of curvature of \(\lambda\), is the radial curvature of the surface, the
curvature along the line of sight;

\[
K_{\mathrm{t}}=1 / r_{\mathrm{t}},
\]
where \(r_{t}\) is the radius of curvature of \(\mu\), is the transverse curvature, the curvature of
the projection. The marvelously simple relation can then be derived (Appendix I):

\[
K_{\mathrm{r}} K_{\mathrm{t}}=K,
\]
where \(K\) denotes the Gaussian curvature of the surface. Before entering into a
discussion on the definition and importance of \(K\), I note another important relation
(Appendix II). If the projection is from a finite distance \(d\), the apparent curvature
of the contour can be found from the simple relation:

\[
K_{\mathrm{app}}=d K_{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{d K}{K_{\mathrm{r}}} .
\]
The meaning of \(K_{\mathrm{app}}\) is that \(\arctan \left(1 / K_{\mathrm{app}}\right)\), expressed in radians, denotes the radius of
the apparent curvature of the contour in the optic array. (The radius of apparent
curvature of the contour is \(\arctan \left(1 / K_{\text {app }}\right) \times 180 / \pi\) deg visual angle.) Thus, the
horizon has a radius of \(90 \operatorname{deg}\left(K_{\mathrm{app}}=0\right)\), whereas the apparent curvature of the
moon's disc is 230 , which is merely the reciprocal of its radius ( 0.25 deg ) in radians.
It may be noted that the apparent curvature of the contour only vanishes at places
where \(K=0\) in realistic cases ( \(K_{\mathrm{r}}<\infty ; d>0\) ). Thus inflections of the contour
correspond to inflections ( \(K=0\) ) of the surface. This is generally true and a
restriction to planar rims and effectively infinite viewing distances is not at all
necessary.
The implications of the above rule are the following. Whenever \(K>0\) the surface
is either a concavity (like the inside of an eggshell) or a convexity (like the outside of
an eggshell). Whenever \(K<0\) the surface is saddle-shaped. For concavities and
convexities the surface is curved in the same sense for all directions, hence the term
synclastic. For a saddle shape, the surface is curved one way for some directions and
the opposite way for other directions: hence the term anticlastic. For a saddle shape
there exist two directions for which a cut through the surface does reveal zero
curvature: these are the asymptotic directions. When the Gaussian curvature vanishes
( \(K=0\) ) the surface is locally cylindrical (curved only in one direction), hence the
term monoclastic. Generically, synclastic and anticlastic points occur in regions,
their boundaries are the flexional curves on which the monoclastic points are
situated. These flexional curves are the surface analogues of the inflections of plane
curves (Hilbert and Cohn-Vossen 1932). They are of great importance in the
Figure 1. (a) A surface seen from a visual direction \(\xi\), the eye at infinity. The shaded region
cannot be seen from that vantage point, its border is the rim. A tangent ray at the rim is not
(in general) perpendicular to the visual direction ( \(\eta\) not perpendicular to \(\xi\) ). (b) The projection of
the rim on a plane \(\pi\), normal to the visual direction. Its curvature is \(K_{t}\). (c) The object cut with
a plane \(\nu\) defined by the visual direction \(\xi\) and the normal at the surface. Its curvature is \(K_{\mathrm{r}}\).
mathematical description of solid shape and are also recognized in artistic practice
(Koenderink and van Doorn 1980, 1982, 1984). In the literature on differential
geometry the reader will often find the terms elliptic, hyperbolic, and parabolic for
synclastic, anticlastic, and monoclastic regions, respectively. I prefer the latter, more
descriptive, terms which also have been in continued use in the mathematical literature
on the study of surfaces in three-dimensional space.
4 The general rules of contour curvature
Consider the consequences of the general formula
First note that the radial curvature \(K_{\mathrm{r}}\) cannot change sign at a contour, because the
object occludes the sight of farther parts (figure 2). Let us treat this curvature as
positive (an arbitrary choice). The distance \(d\) is also a positive number, thus the sign of
\(K_{\text {app }}\) is the same as the sign of the Gaussian curvature.
First consider the case \(K>0\) : concavities and convexities. For a concavity the
case \(K_{\mathrm{r}}>0\) can never occur, thus concavities can never occur at the contour. For
convexities \(K_{\text {app }}>0\) necessarily; let us use this to define convexity of the contour
(figure 2). For the case \(K<0\) there exists only a certain set of visual directions for
which \(K_{\mathrm{r}}>0\). For such cases \(K_{\mathrm{app}}<0\) necessarily; thus the contour displays a
concavity (figure 2).
As a result, the general rule can be stated: convexities of the contour correspond
to convex surface patches, concavities to saddle-shaped surface patches. There are no
exceptions to this rule; it is universally valid for all vantage points and irrespective of
the fact that the rim is, in general, a general space curve. Note that the requirements
of a planar rim and an effectively infinite viewing distance formulated by Marr as
necessary conditions for an interpretation of apparent curvature are in fact quite
unnecessary. This is a happy circumstance because such conditions would limit the
cases that can be treated at all to such a small set as to be without practical use. That
the requirement of infinite viewing distance is actually unnecessary can be understood
without any computation at all: theorems about the sign of the curvature of the
contour are theorems about the contact of the surface with planes and lines, thus
invariant against projective transformations. Any theorem for distant vantage points
can easily be transformed to a theorem about a close vantage point and vice versa.
Figure 2. (a) Conventions of the sign of curvatures used in the text. Left: only a positive radial
curvature leads to a visual contour. Right: a negative apparent curvature is a concavity, a positive
apparent curvature a convexity. (b) Top: the visual direction along an asymptotic ray. The normal cut
is shown, the radial curvature vanishes. The ray touches the surface, yet enters it. Bottom: the same case
in projection: the contour is cusped (infinite apparent curvature), and an ending contour is obtained.
Marr's example of the 'snake' may be modelled with a torus, cut into two and
pasted together again after a transposition (figure 3). For the near part the rim runs
through the synclastic region, for the far part in the anticlastic region. Thus
convexities and concavities of the contour neatly correspond to synclastic and
anticlastic patches of the snake: there is a simple relation between shape of contour
and shape of surface. That artists are sensitive to the transition from one kind of
curvature to the other may be seen from the way the hatching changes in the tail of
the lion in Dürer's "Samson killing the lion" (1498) as shown in figure 4.
The case \(K<0\) permits another possibility that is of great interest: there exist
two directions for which \(K_{\mathrm{r}}\) vanishes: the asymptotic directions. For such a direction
the ray is tangent to the surface, yet it enters the surface (figure 2b). Our general
formula yields \(K_{\text {app }}=\infty\) : an infinite curvature. The contour has a cusp at such a point
Figure 3. (a) A figure taken from Marr (1982). The suggestion is that convexities and concavities
in the projection of the snake have to do with relative distances rather than with local shapes.
(b) A torus cut into two and pasted together again. The shaded regions are anticlastic, the other
regions synclastic. The small insets show the generic case after a small deformation. In projection
(right), this 'snake' has convexities where the body is locally egg-shaped, concavities where the
body is locally saddle-shaped, inflexions at flexional curves of the body.
Figure 4. Details from Dürer's "Samson killing the lion". (Bartsch \#2; the print dates from 1498.)
and only one branch of the cusp is visible (figure 2b). Thus this case corresponds to an
ending contour. The rim is quite regular at such points, without any cusp. The cusp in
the contour is a result of the fact that the rim runs along the ray: this is possible
because the rim is a space curve. For objects with only planar curves as possible rims
(such as a sphere) an ending contour can never be obtained.
5 Implications of planarity and nonplanarity of the rim
Incorrect assumptions about geometrical configurations leading to convexities and
concavities of the contour have led several writers to the notion that an assumption of
planarity of the rim is necessary if one is to make sense of these flexuosities. For
example, Marr (1982, p 222) states: "This third assumption is a strong one that sharply
delimits the class of surfaces whose shape can be interpreted by silhouette. However, it
seems unavoidable if we wish to distinguish convex and concave segments in the
interpretation process". I shall discuss the restrictions created by such an assumption
here, but first of all deal with some further speculations related to this point. It has
been remarked that the planar condition is actually embodied in most modern design.
This is untrue if you include such common shapes as cuboids (the rim for a cube is only
planar for a restricted set of vantage points). It has also been remarked that all of the
outlines drawn in mechanical engineering diagrams satisfy this condition. In fact the
perusal of any book on engineering drawing will reveal many exceptions (for example
figure 5). Moreover, it is held that if the condition of rim planarity is violated, we do
seem to get the shape wrong. As an example it is remarked that a hexagon is not
spontaneously perceived as the projection of a cube. This is certainly true, but it is
also true that those projections of a cube for which the rim is planar are seldom
perceived as projections of a cube, whereas 'generic' projections which show little
symmetry and have nonplanar rims are the most easily interpreted as projections of
cubes. For example, figure 6 shows an illustration from a technical book on drawing
in which the artist is advised to choose generic projections-with nonplanar rims-for
an optimum suggestion of solid shape.
A set of objects which display planar rims for a restricted set of vantage points are
the so-called 'generalized cones', introduced by Binford (1971) and employed by
many authors since. The torus is an example of such an object. It is often bluntly
Figure 5. Examples from technical drawing. These are just some random selections; numerous
examples of nonplanar rims can be found in any book on technical drawing methods. (a) Figure
from the leaflet on Rotring drawing implements. Isometric after DIN 5. (b) Figure from "Audels
Mechanical Drawing Guide" by Audel Staff, Theo. Audel and Cp, Publ., New York, 1947, p 88.)
stated that a generalized cone automatically produces planar rims. This is not the
case; however, an added restriction is that the axis is not foreshortened. For a
general generalized cone, such as the torus, this meant that the rim is planar only
for a single direction of view. This is obvious for a typical projection of the torus
which shows two ending contours (figure 7): at these points the rim runs in the
visual direction, thus if the rim were indeed planar the eye would be in the plane of
that curve and the contour would degenerate to a segment of a great circle of the
optic array which is apparently not the case.
Thus the (unnecessary) restriction to planar rims not only restricts the choice of
objects to generalized cones, it also severely restricts the permitted directions of view
(often to a single one). Thus, almost all visual directions are forbidden. "Stated
baldly, this result means that, in general, shape cannot be derived from occluding
contours alone unless that shape is made from generalized cones and is viewed from
a position from where its axis is not foreshortened" (Marr 1982, p 223). This appears
Figure 6. An instructive figure taken from a book on drawing (Hogarth 1981, p 11). Note how
the strongest spatial impressions arise from the 'generic' views for which the rim is everything but a
plane curve. The original legend runs:
"Geometrical Silhouettes
Some silhouettes are ambiguous and some are not. In the first column at the left, the three
squares are ambiguous because they do not suggest specific three-dimensional forms. It is not
clear whether they represent cubes, a head-on view of a cylinder, or the rectangular base of a
pyramid. The middle column represents a slight shift in viewpoint, with less visual confusion: the
three silhouettes do, in fact, suggest a cubic form, a cylinder, and a pyramid. But the three
silhouettes in the righthand column are clearer still, particularly in the case of the pyramid, which
now obviously rests on a square base".
Figure 7. A typical view of a torus. Note the two ending contours. At these points the rim is
along the visual direction. The rim is a twisted space curve.
to be a meagre basis for the claim that "... a natural link exists between generalized
cones and the imaging process itself" and that "generalized cones will play an
intimate role in the development of vision theory" (Marr 1982, p 223).
It may be that Marr was really looking for minimal constraints that would permit
the computation of global three-dimensional shape from a single projection. This
problem is clearly highly degenerate and any solution would depend more on the
arbitrary constraints (for example, that the shape be a generalized cylinder) than on
the actual data. In contradistinction to the global problem, local shape (that is, the
three-dimensional shape of regions on the rim) can be inferred from the data in a
consistent way.
The facts are that the only shapes which yield planar rims for any vantage point
are the developable surfaces (planes, cylinders, cones, tangent surfaces of space
curves) and the quadrics (the ellipsoids, paraboloids, and hyperboloids). This has
been proved by Blaschke (1923).
6 Conclusions
In this paper I have discussed the geometrical facts about the implications of the
concavities and convexities of visual contours for the inference of the local shape of
surface regions. I have done this with the help of a simple but powerful theorem
about surface curvatures that appears to be novel. It is intimately related to two
results of differential geometry: one well-known and mentioned in any elementary
treatise, namely Euler's theorem; the other rather obscure and mentioned in only a
few texts, namely Blaschke's "dual" of Euler's theorem. (The theorem \(K_{\mathrm{t}} K_{\mathrm{r}}=K\)
does not appear in any text I know of, however.) The result is very simple and
clear-cut: for any vantage point and without any restriction on the shape of the rim,
a convexity of the contour corresponds to a convex patch of the surface, and a
concavity to a saddle-shaped patch. Inflections of the contour correspond to the
flexional curves of the surface. Thus the flexuosities of the contour signify actual
properties of the surface, a fact that does not seem to have been appreciated by
workers in the field of vision before: although I have quoted some remarks from
Marr's (1982) text, I could have used many other sources.
The theorem about the relation between the radial and the projected curvatures is
of interest in another way, not noted above: because the Gaussian curvature is
invariant against arbitrary bending of the surface, the product of these curvatures is
also invariant against bending. Thus the theorem can be extended to a class of
nonrigid shapes. Here, 'bending' means any transformation that conserves the metric
as measured along the surface. Thus paper and flexible steel shells can easily be
subjected to bendings, but with rubber membranes care must be taken to avoid
stretching. As a result one has the theorem that arbitrary bendings cannot change
convexities of the contour into concavities and vice versa. (This can easily be verified
if one cuts a cheap rubber football into two. Through bending one can easily
change the radial and transverse curvature over a certain range, but one cannot
produce a concave contour without stretching.) The apparent curvature of the
contour really enables one to infer intrinsic properties of the surface geometry.

Appendix
1 Projected and radial curvature
Consider first the case of a distant (effectively infinitely distant) vantage point, that
is, parallel projection. Let the direction of sight be the \(x\) direction. Consider a
point P on the rim, and the \(z\) direction in the direction of the normal. Then the
\(x-y\) plane is the tangent plane at P . Let P be the origin of the Cartesian coordinate
system, then one has as description of the surface near P :

\[
z(x, y)=\frac{1}{2}\left(a x^{2}+2 b x y+c y^{2}\right)+(\text { third-order and higher terms }) .
\]
The locus of the rim is defined through the condition

\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}=a x+b y+(\text { second-order and higher terms })=0,
\]
because the \(x\) direction grazes the surface at the rim. Note that the rim runs in
the direction that makes an angle \(\varphi=\arctan (-a / b)\) with the positive \(x\) direction.
Thus the rim is not, in general, perpendicular to the line of sight; one says it is
"conjugated" to that direction.
When \(x\) is eliminated from equations (A1) and (A2), one obtains
(neglecting third-order and higher terms), which is the analytical expression that
describes the projection of the rim on the \(y-z\) plane, in other words, the contour.
The curvature of the projection is thus
The normal plane through the line of sight is the \(x-z\) plane. The surface meets
this plane in the curve
if third-order and higher terms are neglected. Thus the radial curvature is

\[
K_{\mathrm{r}}=a
\]
From equations (A4) and (A6) one obtains immediately the important result

\[
K_{\mathrm{r}} K_{\mathrm{t}}=a c-b^{2}=\frac{\left(\partial^{2} z / \partial x^{2}\right)\left(\partial^{2} z / \partial y^{2}\right)-\left(\partial^{2} z / \partial x \partial y\right)^{2}}{1+(\partial z / \partial x)^{2}+(\partial z / \partial y)^{2}}=K
\]
as \(\partial z / \partial x=\partial z / \partial y=0\) in P and \(\partial^{2} z / \partial x^{2}=a, \partial^{2} z / \partial y^{2}=c\), and \(\partial^{2} z / \partial x \partial y=b\).
2 The apparent curvature
Let \(\xi\) be a tangent ray through P , and let Q be any point on \(\xi\). Let the distance PQ
be \(d\). From Q other tangent rays may be drawn; these rays are the generators of a
cone with vertex Q that touches the surface along the rim. Consider the second-order
osculating cone, the second-order cone that approximates the real one most closely.
When one lets Q range over \(\xi\), the semitop angle of this osculating cone varies. This
always occurs in a neat, predictable manner, however, A useful theorem, originally
due to Mannheim but first well described by Blaschke (1916), states: all osculating
cones envelop a sphere. This sphere may be called the 'Mannheim sphere'.
When Q is at infinity ( \(d \rightarrow \infty\) ) the cone degenerates into a cylinder with radius \(K_{\mathrm{t}}^{-1}\).
This must also be the radius of the Mannheim sphere, for a cylinder touches a sphere
along a great circle.
Consider figure A1. The Mannheim sphere at P is drawn, with radius \(R_{\mathrm{t}}=K_{\mathrm{t}}^{-1}\).
The sphere obviously touches the tangent plane, thus the normal to the sphere is the
normal to the surface (at P ). Also drawn is an osculating cone for a point Q at
distance \(d\). From simple geometry one obtains the semitop angle, \(\alpha\) :

\[
\alpha=\arctan \left(\frac{1}{d K_{\mathrm{t}}}\right) .
\]
Around Q is constructed a unit sphere which represents the manifold of visual
directions for the point Q . The osculating cone cuts this unit sphere in a small circle.
The (spherical) curvature of this small circle is the apparent curvature of the contour.
To calculate the apparent curvature one must find the geodesic curvature of this
small circle. It can be shown (for example, Lipschutz 1969) to be given by

\[
K_{\mathrm{app}}=\cot \alpha .
\]
Consequently, one obtains

\[
K_{\mathrm{app}}=d K_{\mathrm{t}},
\]
that is, the formula used in the text. (Thus the radius of apparent curvature is
\(K_{\text {app }}^{-1}=\left(d K_{\mathrm{t}}\right)^{-1}\) radians, or \(180 / \pi d K_{\mathrm{t}}\) deg visual angle.)
Figure A1.
